old_claim.note: Laboratory Diagnostic Medicine provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Dialysis machines are clinical renal-failure treatment devices; the 1943 artificial kidney was used to treat patients rather than as a diagnostic lab method.

source_shape.source_locator: Kolff and Scribner discussion of renal failure, artificial kidneys, and patient treatments beginning in 1943.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source frames the artificial kidney as a treatment for renal failure, describes patient use in Kampen, and later describes durable hemodialysis as clinical life support.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports clinical medicine as the contextual enabler while rejecting laboratory diagnostics as the direct field parent because the device lineage is therapeutic patient care.

ontology_before: Dialysis machines were connected to laboratory diagnostic medicine, making a treatment device look like a diagnostic-lab technique.
ontology_after: Dialysis machines are connected to clinical medicine as a therapeutic care context, with membranes and pump/flow architecture as technical foundations.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: laboratory diagnostic medicine is absent and clinical medicine is the enabling clinical context.
would_reject_if: The node were re-scoped to laboratory dialysis assays rather than patient dialysis machines.
A source showed clinical dialysis machines originated primarily as diagnostic laboratory instruments.
The graph removed the membrane method dependency while changing the clinical context.
short_reason: Dialysis machines are treatment devices, not diagnostic laboratory methods.
